description: This figure presents the phase evolution and precipitate characterization of a novel 30Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr duplex low-density steel annealed at 600–1050 °C. Panel (a) displays X-ray diffraction patterns revealing the temperature-dependent phase transformation: the cold-rolled (CR) structure contains austenite (FCC) and ferrite (BCC); upon annealing, β-Mn forms at 700–900 °C, while Cr7C3 carbides and κ-carbides appear at 950–1000 °C, and dissolve at 1050 °C. Panels (b) and (c) provide transmission electron microscopy evidence. Subfigure (b) shows DP950-5 (950 °C, 5 min) with Cr7C3 carbides precipitating at grain boundaries, confirmed by the [0001] zone axis SAED pattern. Subfigure (c) depicts DP1000-5 (1000 °C, 5 min), where ferrite and austenite coexist; high-resolution imaging identifies ultrafine intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles within austenite (radius 0.86 ± 0.20 nm, volume fraction 1.56 %), with corresponding SAED patterns confirming the austenite/κ-carbide orientation relationship. These results demonstrate that annealing at 1000 °C dissolves brittle intergranular carbides while precipitating strengthening intragranular nanoparticles, achieving an optimal microstructure.
